subject: Electrotherapy II
definition: Interferential current is the resultant current produced when two or more alternating currents are applied simultaneously at the point of intersection in a given medium.
Interferential therapy uses two medium frequency currents to produce a low frequency effect within tissues.
principle: Interferential therapy depends upon the principle of interference of two medium frequency currents crossing within the patient's tissues.
One current is usually maintained at 4000 Hz.
The second current is varied according to treatment requirements.
At the point where the currents cross, a new current is produced.
The amplitude of the resultant current is modulated.
Beat frequency is produced by the difference between the two carrier frequencies.
Example: 4000 Hz and 3900 Hz produce a beat frequency of 100 Hz.
Beat frequencies from 1–250 Hz can be produced by varying the carrier frequency difference.

parameters.sweepFrequency: Frequency directly fed to the beat frequency by the machine
parameters.spectrum: Range of frequencies automatically traversed during treatment to prevent accommodation
parameters.balance: Used to equalize current flow in both channels
parameters.impedance: Resistance, capacitance and inductance collectively offered by tissues
parameters.phase: Relationship between currents traveling in same or opposite phase

apparatusPreparation: Ensure all knobs are at zero
skinPreparation: Cover lesions with petroleum jelly if present
electrodePlacement: Electrodes are placed in two pairs
Electrodes are positioned diagonally to each other
Crossing point should lie over or around the affected part
treatmentProcedure: Equalize current using balance control if required

precautions: Skin should be clean before treatment
Advise patient to report discomfort immediately
Equalize current if channel imbalance occurs
Do not allow patient to pull leads
Do not allow patient to touch surrounding objects during treatment
clinicalPearls: One channel is usually fixed at 4000 Hz.
Beat frequency equals the difference between carrier frequencies.
IFT produces low frequency effects using medium frequency currents.
Skin resistance is lower at 4000 Hz than with low frequency currents.
Four-electrode quadripolar application is the common method.
80–100 Hz rhythmic frequency is commonly used for pain relief.
1–10 Hz rhythmic frequency is used for absorption of exudates.
Balance control equalizes unequal channel currents.

clinicalProtocols.electrodePlacement: Four electrodes in two diagonal pairs
Four electrodes in two diagonal pairs around knee

clinicalProtocols.purpose: Pumping action and reduction of swelling

clinicalProtocols.position: Supine lying with hip and knee flexed
